attributes.stem:
A 72-year-old man presents to his GP with a four-month history of progressively worsening left calf pain. The ‘cramping’ pain mainly occurs when he walks more than 200 metres on a flat surface and comes on earlier when walking on an incline. It is relieved by rest. His medical history includes hypertension, type II diabetes mellitus and previous resection of a melanoma in the right leg. His medications include candesartan and metformin. He has a 40 pack-year smoking history and drinks two to three standard drinks daily.

On examination, the right femoral, popliteal, dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial pulses are easily felt. Only the left femoral and popliteal pulses are palpable. There is bilateral lower limb loss of protective sensation however motor function is normal. Straight leg raising test was negative bilaterally. There is no tissue loss. Toe pressures (obtained from the hallux) are 100 mmHg on the right and 48 mmHg on the left.
attributes.stemQuestion: Given the most likely diagnosis, which one of the following is the most appropriate investigation to perform next?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with symptoms consistent with intermittent claudication in the left leg. He has modifiable and non-modifiable vascular risk factors that include his age, sex, hypertension and T2DM. The history of calf pain on walking/exertion that is relieved by rest is typical of this condition, with physical examination findings further supporting this diagnosis.

Exercise ankle-brachial pulse index (ABPI) is initiated if the symptoms and clinical findings require distinction between arterial and neurogenic aetiology. Resting ABPI are measured and then measured once again after a treadmill exercise test. A significant drop in ABPI indicates the presence of haemodynamically significant arterial disease. ABPI is not routinely used in patients with diabetes and/or chronic kidney disease due to arterial wall calcification that results in falsely elevated measurements. Toe pressures or Toe brachial indices are commonly used as a more reliable quantitative perfusion measurement.

Arterial duplex ultrasound scan (DUS) is the correct option. The advantages of DUS are it is non-invasive, low cost, no iodinated contrast media, no ionising radiation, and no fixed installation. The aim of DUS is to look for and to localise and characterise a site of arterial obstruction.

MR angiogram and CT angiogram are good imaging modalities but are more invasive compared to ultrasonography. These definitive tests are indicated when planning surgical intervention or if ultrasound has not been effective in demonstrating the suspected arterial disease.

CT or MRI lumbo-sacral spine is a useful investigative tool if neurogenic claudication is suspected a result of spinal stenosis. Patients will often have a history of back pain, as well as shooting pain in the leg/buttock (rather than a cramping) that is more likely to occur with positional changes (often lumbar extension) rather than exertion. Motor weakness may also be present. The straight leg raising test in this patient was negative making the diagnosis of spinal pathology less likely.

Given the clinical picture is strongly suggestive of peripheral vascular disease, the patient should be commenced on best medical therapy (antiplatelet and statin therapy), optimisation of his diabetic control and lifestyle modification that includes smoking cessation and and exercise should be initiated.
learningPoints: Exercise ankle-brachial pulse index (ABPI) is initiated if the symptoms and clinical findings require distinction between arterial and neurogenic aetiology.
references: Conte MS, Bradbury AW, Kolh P, White JV, Dick F, Fitridge R, et al. Global vascular guidelines on the management of chronic limb-threatening ischemia. J Vasc Surg. 2019 Jun;69(6S):3S-125S.e40.

Aboyans V, Ricco J-B, Bartelink M-LEL, Björck M, Brodmann M, Cohnert T, et al. 2017 ESC Guidelines on the Diagnosis and Treatment of Peripheral Arterial Diseases, in collaboration with the European Society for Vascular Surgery (ESVS): Document covering atherosclerotic disease of extracranial carotid and vertebral, mesenteric, renal, upper and lower extremity arteries. European Heart Journal. 2017;39(9) 763- 816. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/28886620/
